title: Celtis glabrata
intro: Celtis glabrata, also known as the Mexican hackberry or Mexican sugarberry, is a deciduous shrub or small tree native to Mexico and Central America, belonging to the family Cannabaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its potential health benefits, including treating digestive issues, fever, and respiratory problems, as well as its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Celtis glabrata include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Celtis glabrata include infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, often used to treat colds, coughs, and other respiratory issues.
taxonomy: Celtis glabrata is classified within the family Cannabaceae, but it is often placed in the family Ulmaceae or the family Cannabaceae in different sources. The genus Celtis is a part of the order Rosales. It belongs to the class Magnoliopsida, also known as the flowering plants, and the division Magnoliophyta. At the kingdom level, it is classified as Plantae. Its classification may vary based on the taxonomic system used.
uses: Celtis glabrata has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. It is believed to have anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which may contribute to its potential health benefits. In some cultures, the plant is used to treat fever, cough, and respiratory issues. Additionally, its extracts may have wound-healing and antiseptic properties, making it useful for skin care and wound treatment. Further research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential applications of Celtis glabrata.
benefits: Celtis glabrata, also known as the netleaf hackberry or netleaf hackberry tree, has been traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties.
